narrative: Your law school application was denied because our predictive model, which assesses the likelihood of first-time success on the bar exam, estimated a 96% chance that you would not pass it. This high predicted risk of failure directly led to the rejection, as the university prioritizes admitting students with strong indicators of bar passage to maintain their outcomes. The model's decision for you, as a Black male applicant, hinged most heavily on your academic decile rankings from school—specifically, your Year 3 decile of 1, which is well below the typical student's rank around the middle of their class (average 5.5). This low Year 3 performance stood out as the strongest factor pushing the prediction toward failure, signaling to the model a potential struggle with the rigorous demands ahead. Next in influence was your Year 1 decile, also at 1 compared to an average of about 5.7, which reinforced concerns about consistent early academic challenges. Your LSAT score of 27, notably lower than the dataset average of 36.7, added further weight toward the failure prediction, as it highlighted a gap in standardized test readiness relative to peers. While factors like your middle family income bracket and full-time student status (common among 93% of applicants) played lesser roles, they didn't offset these core academic signals. In contrast to typical admitted students, who often enter with higher deciles and LSATs closer to or above average, your profile aligned more closely with patterns seen in those who struggled on the bar. This isn't a judgment on your potential or background—many from similar circumstances succeed with targeted effort—but the model focuses on these measurable predictors. To move forward, consider retaking the LSAT to boost your score, strengthening your undergraduate record through additional coursework or a post-bac program, and seeking mentorship from bar prep programs tailored for underrepresented students like yourself. Building a stronger academic foundation now could dramatically shift future predictions in your favor. With persistence, you're positioning yourself for law school success down the line.
